text: Hello friends, hope all of you are doing well. This is a video presentation of laparoscopic management of a case of adhesive band obstruction. A seventy five year old male with history of intermittent abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, chronic constipation for five years. He had a history of multiple hospital admissions with each time managed conservatively. The history of open cholecystectomy eight years back.

Known case of diabetic hypertensive Parkinson's disease. Now this time he was admitted under me with same symptoms with a history of fracture neck femur due to fall in toilet. CT scan revealed small bowel obstruction due to band additions. Other workups are normal. So our plan is to go ahead with laparoscopy and proceed.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic approach to identify omental adhesions near the previous cholecystectomy scar, starting from the iliac fossa. The cecum and appendix are visible, and a band connecting the terminal ileum to the mesentery is identified. The band is divided, and omental adhesions are released from the anterior abdominal wall using harmonic shears for better exposure.

The surgeon begins tracing the bowel from the ileocecal junction, using atraumatic graspers to gently handle the bowel loops. The terminal ileum is identified, and a bowel walk is performed from distal to proximal. The ileal loops appear collapsed, suggesting a proximal obstruction. A mesenteric cyst is visible in the jejunum, and an adhesive band is observed connecting the mesentery of the small bowel.

The surgeon identifies the main site of obstruction in the bowel, noting two adhesive bands: one connecting the terminal ileum with the transverse colon, and another at the mid jejunal level. The latter band, which has been divided, caused a constriction ring leading to intermittent obstruction. The jejunum proximal to the divided band appears dilated, indicating a zone of transition. The surgeon plans to proceed proximally towards the duodenojejunal flexure to check for additional bands.

The surgeon carefully handles the dilated and congested bowel loops using atraumatic bowel graspers, avoiding undue traction to prevent serosal tears, while tracing the bowel proximally towards the duodenojejunal flexure.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position from head down to head up while exploring the left upper quadrant of the abdomen. The transverse colon is visible, along with the divided portion of an adhesive band that previously connected the transverse colon to the terminal ileum. The terminal ileum is observed with a constriction ring encircling it, indicating a site of obstruction.

The surgeon uses harmonic shears to dissect and divide the constriction ring connected to the adhesive band, ensuring the active blade is kept away from the bowel surface. The band is carefully dissected and its position confirmed before division, focusing on the proximal jejunal aspect while tracing the bowel.
The surgeon reaches the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure, identifying it as a landmark with the inferior mesenteric vein (IME) visible. The procedure utilizes three 5mm ports.

So soft diet was allowed on day two and patient underwent hip replacement surgery on day five. So laparoscopy is a fantastic tool in selected cases of intestinal obstruction. The morbidity of laparotomy can easily be avoided so patient can get a early recovery and optimize and plan judiciously. thank you for watching my videos and don't forget to subscribe to see more videos thank you
